title: HumorRank: A Tournament-Based Leaderboard for Evaluating Humor Generation in Large Language Models
research_questions: Can pairwise tournament judgments aggregated with Bradley–Terry estimation yield a scalable, globally consistent leaderboard for textual humor generation?
Do independent LLM judges produce stable humor-generation rankings across SemEval-2026 MWAHAHA and Humor Transfer Bench?
How much comparison budget can Adaptive Swiss Pairing save while preserving rank fidelity relative to full round-robin evaluation?
Which humor mechanisms, delivery features, and failure modes distinguish frontier, specialist, mid-tier, and baseline models?
How do LLM judge preferences compare with blind human humor preference judgments?
tasks: humor mechanism, delivery-feature, and failure-mode tagging

datasets_used.name: Humor Transfer Bench (HTB)
datasets_used.size: 400 headline prompts held out from the SemEval distribution

methods_proposed.name: HumorRank
methods_proposed.summary: A tournament-based leaderboard framework in which contestant LLMs generate jokes for shared prompts, same-prompt pairwise duels are judged by an LLM, and global model rankings are estimated with Bradley–Terry maximum likelihood. The framework also reports Stable Elo scores as a sequence-robust audit metric and produces interpretable humor-feature profiles.

methods_proposed.name: GTVH-grounded pairwise LLM-judge protocol
methods_proposed.summary: A structured A/B/TIE judging prompt asks an LLM comedy critic to choose the funnier joke, provide brief reasoning, and emit winner humor mechanisms, delivery features, and loser failure tags. The feature lists are grounded in the General Theory of Verbal Humor and include mechanisms such as incongruity, wordplay, absurdity, surprise, irony, and sarcasm.

methods_proposed.name: Adaptive Swiss Pairing
methods_proposed.summary: A budget-aware tournament scheduler that preferentially matches models of similar temporary standing while avoiding repeated or over-sampled pairings. Full budget recovers full round-robin, while Swiss 2RR and Swiss 3RR reduce comparisons to fixed-round schedules before final ratings are computed with global Bradley–Terry MLE.

methods_proposed.name: Stable Elo audit
methods_proposed.summary: A sequential Elo reference metric that reduces order dependence by evaluating the complete match history across N=10 randomly shuffled topological orderings and averaging terminal ratings.

humor_theories.detail: The pairwise judge protocol emits structured humor mechanism, delivery, and failure tags grounded in GTVH resources such as Logical Mechanism, Situation, Target, and Language.
The paper defines humor as cognitive reward arising when an interlocutor resolves a deliberately constructed incongruity, and uses pairwise preference ranking to compare the strength of this reward.
Appendix A states that the humor definition requires a harmless and non-threatening context, drawn from benign violation theory.
Related work lists superiority among classical theories explaining humor.
Related work lists relief among classical theories explaining humor.
Appendix A cites Normative-Violation theory (Veatch, 1998) as part of the working definition of humor.

evaluation_methods.detail: Primary pairwise judge is Llama 3.3 70B Instruct; secondary validation judge is Qwen 2.5 72B Instruct. For each same-prompt duel, the judge selects A, B, or TIE, returns brief reasoning, and tags GTVH humor mechanisms, delivery features, and failure modes; prompt order is swapped across comparisons.

Global rankings use Bradley–Terry MLE with MM convergence ε < 10^-6, ratings anchored at 1000, and 95% confidence intervals from 200 bootstrap resamples (seed 42). Stable Elo uses K-factor 32 and 10 shuffled orderings; analyses also report Kendall τ, Krippendorff α, win rates, binomial tests, and transitivity score.

A blind human annotation study re-rated 60 stratified SemEval pairs. Three invited annotators with native or near-native English proficiency and prior coursework or research exposure to humor and NLP produced 175 votes; 55 pairs have votes from all three.

headline_results.finding: On the primary SemEval full round-robin leaderboard judged by Llama 3.3 70B, GPT-5 ranked first and clearly separated from the field.
headline_results.metric: BT Rating / Stable Elo / Win Rate
headline_results.value: BT Rating 1307.5; Stable Elo 1317.6; 95% CL [1289.9, 1325.9]; Win Rate 84.0%

headline_results.finding: The specialized HumorGen-7B ranked fourth on the primary SemEval leaderboard, above substantially larger general-purpose models such as GPT OSS 120B.
headline_results.value: HumorGen-7B BT = 1092.8, Win Rate 59.8%; GPT OSS 120B BT = 1015.0, Win Rate 49.8%

headline_results.finding: On HTB full round-robin with the Llama judge, GPT-5 remained first and HumorGen-7B rose to third.
headline_results.value: GPT-5 BT = 1314.7, Win Rate 84.1%; HumorGen-7B BT = 1097.7, Win Rate 60.6%

headline_results.finding: Independent Llama and Qwen LLM judges produced highly stable rankings on both benchmarks.
headline_results.value: Kendall τ = 0.889 (p = 0.0002) in each benchmark; pooled 25,200 labels: 82.9% agreement; Krippendorff α = 0.658
headline_results.dataset: SemEval-2026 Task 1 MWAHAHA data; Humor Transfer Bench (HTB)

headline_results.finding: Adaptive Swiss Pairing reduced comparison cost while retaining much of the cross-judge rank signal.
headline_results.metric: Pairs per prompt / budget / average cross-LLM-judge τ
headline_results.value: Full RR: 36 pairs/prompt, 100%, τ = 0.889; Swiss 3RR: 12 pairs/prompt, ~33%, τ = 0.861; Swiss 2RR: 8 pairs/prompt, ~22%, τ = 0.806
headline_results.dataset: SemEval-2026 Task 1 MWAHAHA data; Humor Transfer Bench (HTB)

headline_results.finding: Human agreement was moderate, and LLM judge agreement on the same 60-pair sample was comparable to human-only agreement.
headline_results.value: Humans (n=2) α = 0.432; Humans (n=3) α = 0.397; Llama–Qwen α = 0.425; human–human agreement 47.1%; human–Llama agreement 49.7%; Fisher exact p = 0.667

key_findings: GPT-5 ranked first on both SemEval and HTB under the Llama judge, while Base Qwen 7B and Llama 3.3 70B occupied the bottom two ranks in the main SemEval leaderboard.
HumorGen-7B remained competitive with much larger general-purpose systems, ranking fourth on SemEval under the Llama judge and third on HTB under the Llama judge.
Llama 3.3 70B and Qwen 2.5 72B judges produced cross-judge stable orderings with Kendall τ = 0.889 on both benchmarks, with only modest mid-tier reordering.
Swiss 3RR used about one-third of full round-robin comparisons and preserved top and bottom anchor ranks, while Swiss 2RR increased mid-tier volatility.

Feature tags suggested different comedic signatures: GPT-5 emphasized Conciseness (31.1%) and Incongruity (26.5%), HumorGen-7B led on Absurdity (25.9%), Sarcasm (9.2%), and Escalation (14.5%), and Llama 70B over-indexed on Wordplay (29.5%) while accumulating Weak Punchline (41.1%) and Cliché (50.9%) loser tags.

The human study found no statistically significant difference between pooled human–human agreement (47.1%) and human–Llama judge agreement (49.7%; Fisher exact p = 0.667), supporting the LLM judge protocol only as directional evidence rather than ground truth.
limitations: Evaluation is monolingual and conducted only on English data; cross-lingual and cross-cultural humor settings are not examined.
Experiments are limited to nine contestant models and do not exhaust the full space of contemporary systems.
The benchmarks cover headline-conditioned textual humor and do not cover interactive or multimodal humor paradigms.
LLM judges may inherit cultural and stylistic biases from pretraining, and rejected judge configurations showed self-preference or family bias.
GTVH feature tags are co-produced with duel judgments but were not separately human-audited.
The paper does not claim a formal convergence proof for Bradley–Terry estimation under Adaptive Swiss Pairing; fidelity is supported empirically.
The blind human evaluation is small, covering 60 SemEval pairs with three annotators and 55 pairs with full three-way overlap.
safety_ethics_notes: The ethics statement emphasizes that humor is culturally and contextually variable and can overlap with offensive or exclusionary expression, so downstream practitioners must enforce appropriate content moderation. It also warns that LLM judges trained on high-resource, Western-centric text may disadvantage humor conventions from underrepresented linguistic or cultural communities, so HumorRank should be treated as a reproducible diagnostic benchmark rather than a definitive measure of comedic quality.

artifacts.other: Live leaderboard: https://humorrank-leaderboard.pages.dev/. The paper states that tournament code, evaluation scripts, and the Adaptive Swiss pairing implementation are provided in the supplementary materials.

human_samples.population: Invited Master’s students with native or near-native English proficiency and prior coursework or research exposure to humor and NLP
human_samples.recruitment: Recruited by invitation; completed the task without payment
human_samples.observations: 175 nominal A/B/Tie votes across 60 SemEval pairs; 55 pairs have votes from all three annotators
human_samples.detail: Blind human annotation study. Annotators saw the headline and two anonymized jokes, with model identities hidden and left/right order randomized per pair. The paper reports human-only dyad and three-rater Krippendorff alpha, plus human–LLM judge agreement.

judges.name: Llama 3.3 70B Instruct
judges.detail: Primary production pairwise judge for SemEval and HTB full round-robin tournaments. For each same-prompt duel, it selected A, B, or TIE, returned brief reasoning, and emitted winner humor-mechanism tags, delivery-feature tags, and loser failure tags. The prompt template contains the headline and Joke A/B, not model identities. Also used in abandoned numeric-scoring judge experiments.

judges.name: Qwen 2.5 72B Instruct
judges.detail: Secondary production LLM judge used to replicate full round-robin duels and validate cross-judge rank stability; also used for Qwen-judge feature and failure-mode distributions and HTB Swiss budget tables.

judges.name: GPT-5
judges.detail: Rejected pairwise screening judge in Appendix B. On five shared SemEval headlines, GPT-5-as-judge showed 87.5% self-win when judging its own model’s output and was not used for production rankings.

judges.name: Gemini 2.5 Pro
judges.detail: Rejected judge configuration in Appendix B, including a 120-headline absolute-scoring pilot and pairwise screening on five shared SemEval headlines. Gemini-as-judge showed 87.5% self-win and was not used for production rankings.

judges.name: GPT-OSS 120B
judges.detail: Rejected pairwise screening judge in Appendix B. The paper reports approximately 75% self-win in the screening runs and excludes it from production judging.

judges.name: Qwen3-32B
judges.detail: Rejected pairwise screening judge in Appendix B. It ranked itself fourth with approximately 57.5% self-win and was not used for production judging.

judges.name: Three invited annotators H1–H3
judges.detail: Human validators re-rated 60 stratified SemEval duels with anonymized joke options. Their labels were used to compute human-only, human–Llama, and human–Qwen agreement statistics, not to construct the main leaderboard.

judges.name: Bradley–Terry maximum-likelihood estimation
judges.detail: Primary leaderboard aggregation method. Fits global BT ratings from pairwise outcomes using MM convergence epsilon < 1e-6, ratings anchored at 1000, with 95% confidence intervals from 200 bootstrap resamples using seed 42.

judges.name: Stable Elo
judges.detail: Sequence-robust audit metric using K-factor 32 and N=10 randomly shuffled orderings of the match history; reported alongside BT ratings.

judges.name: Kendall tau, Krippendorff alpha, agreement rates, binomial tests, transitivity score
judges.detail: Statistical analyses used to quantify cross-judge rank stability, human/LLM agreement, pairwise separation, and tournament consistency.

generation_selection.scope: Production joke generation for nine contestant models on SemEval-2026 MWAHAHA and HTB prompts
generation_selection.detail: The system prompt asks each candidate model to generate a funny joke and output only the joke text. The paper does not report best-of-N generation or post-generation selection before tournament judging.

generation_selection.scope: Appendix B judge-configuration screening candidate pools
generation_selection.n: Track A reports approximately 11–15 humor candidates per headline in numeric-scoring pilots; Exp. 4 reports 4 models per headline
generation_selection.detail: These pilots were used to evaluate failed judge paradigms. The paper reports scoring or pairwise evaluation of candidate pools but does not fully specify how those candidates were generated or selected.

model_provenance.name: GPT
model_provenance.detail: Contestant model in the production tournament and rejected pairwise screening judge. The paper does not report provider endpoint, API snapshot, deployment ID, or inference date.

model_provenance.name: Gemini
model_provenance.detail: Contestant model and rejected judge configuration in Appendix B. No exact API snapshot, endpoint, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: Claude
model_provenance.detail: Contestant model. No API snapshot, endpoint, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: Kimi
model_provenance.detail: Contestant model. No exact deployment, endpoint, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: Qwen
model_provenance.detail: Contestant model and rejected pairwise screening judge. The paper does not report checkpoint hash, hosting setup, or inference date.

model_provenance.name: GPT-OSS
model_provenance.detail: Contestant model and rejected pairwise screening judge. The paper does not report checkpoint hash, hosting setup, or inference date.

model_provenance.name: Llama
model_provenance.detail: Contestant model and primary production judge. The paper says full round-robin judging on SemEval and HTB required approximately 48 hours of dedicated NVIDIA H100 80GB GPU compute for the primary Llama judge pipeline; it does not report checkpoint hash or inference date.

model_provenance.name: HumorGen
model_provenance.detail: Specialized contestant model described as a Qwen-7B specialist trained via Cognitive Synergy Fusion and supervised fine-tuning in the HumorGen pipeline. The paper cites a Hugging Face URL but does not report checkpoint hash or inference date.

model_provenance.name: Base Qwen
model_provenance.detail: Zero-shot architecture-control contestant. The exact Qwen generation/checkpoint and inference setup are not specified.

model_provenance.name: Qwen
model_provenance.detail: Secondary production LLM judge for cross-judge validation. Described as an open-weight judge, but the paper does not report checkpoint hash, hardware/access setup, or inference date.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: 1 generation call per model–prompt is implied by the single-output prompt; total candidate-generation calls are not explicitly reported
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 1 joke per model per prompt; nine contestant models on 300 SemEval prompts and 400 HTB prompts
inference_budgets.token_budget: Appendix G Table 19 reports Max New Tokens = 256; the Reproducibility Statement also says token limits were inherited from respective model APIs without artificial truncation
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Temperature T = 0.7; top-p = 0.9 set explicitly wherever the provider API exposes it; system prompt: “You are a joke generator. Given a headline or topic, generate a funny joke. Output ONLY the joke text. No thinking tags, no reasoning, no explanation, no extra words.”
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Candidate-generation compute and monetary cost are not separately reported; the paper mentions practical compute and API budget constraints
inference_budgets.detail: Production generation covers headline-conditioned textual jokes for SemEval-2026 MWAHAHA and HTB.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: One JSON evaluation call per scheduled pairwise duel per LLM judge; SemEval Full RR = 10,800 calls per judge; HTB Full RR = 14,400 calls per judge; 25,200 pairwise calls per judge across both benchmarks
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 2 jokes per duel, with A/B/TIE output; K=9 gives 36 unordered model pairs per prompt
inference_budgets.token_budget: Judge Max New Tokens = 512; maximum retry threshold = 3; backoff base = 2.0; retry cap = 4.0
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Judge temperature T = 0.1; structured JSON judge prompt; prompt order is described as swapped across comparisons to mitigate position bias
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Approximately 48 hours of dedicated NVIDIA H100 80GB GPU compute for the primary Llama judge pipeline over SemEval and HTB; Qwen replication required additional inference budget; no monetary cost reported
inference_budgets.detail: Primary judge was Llama 3.3 70B Instruct; secondary validation judge was Qwen 2.5 72B Instruct.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: If run directly: Swiss 3RR = 12 pairs/prompt and Swiss 2RR = 8 pairs/prompt. SemEval: 3,600 and 2,400 judgments; HTB: 4,800 and 3,200 judgments
inference_budgets.token_budget: Same judge token budget as production judging when LLM labels are used
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Same pairwise judge prompt and T = 0.1 judging setup; ASP uses temporary scores only for pairing and final ratings use BT MLE
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: No separate compute or cost reported; several appendix figures describe Swiss runs as simulated/subsampled from the full setting
inference_budgets.detail: Full RR is 36 pairs/prompt. Swiss 3RR is treated as the recommended reduced-budget mode; Swiss 2RR is treated as a minimum-budget stress test.

inference_budgets.scope: Judge-configuration screening and failed judge paradigms
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Numeric Track A: 17,317 individual scores for Exp. 1, approximately 12k scores for Exp. 2, approximately 12k scores for Exp. 3, and a 120-headline Gemini absolute pilot with 4 models/headline. Pairwise Track B: GPT-5 and Gemini judges each evaluated 180 duels on five shared headlines; GPT-OSS and Qwen32 used ten headlines for additional coverage
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: Numeric pilots used approximately 11–15 candidates per headline in Exp. 1–3; pairwise pilots used 2 jokes per duel
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Failed paradigms included absolute 0–100 scoring, structured 4×1–5 scoring, scalar 1–20 funniness scoring, and an early verbose chain-of-thought pairwise prompt
inference_budgets.detail: These runs justified abandoning absolute scoring, rejecting self-preferring proprietary/same-family judges, and revising the pairwise prompt to reduce spurious ties.

inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: BT MM convergence epsilon < 1e-6; ratings anchored at 1000; 200 bootstrap resamples with seed 42; Stable Elo K-factor 32 with 10 shuffled orderings
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: No compute time reported for rating/statistical computation
inference_budgets.detail: Automatic aggregation and analysis rather than model inference.

human_baselines.description: The paper does not include human-generated jokes as contestants and does not compare model jokes against human-authored jokes under matched tournament conditions.
human_baselines.detail: Human input is used for validation of judge agreement, not as a generation baseline.

human_baselines.description: Three blind human annotators re-rated the same 60 anonymized SemEval duels used for comparison with Llama and Qwen judge labels.
human_baselines.detail: Human–human pooled agreement was 47.1%; human–Llama agreement was 49.7% with Fisher exact p = 0.667; Llama–Qwen agreement on the same 60 pairs was 50.0% with alpha = 0.425. This is a directly matched comparison for preference-label agreement, not for generation quality.

mitigations: HTB is described as 400 headline prompts held out from the SemEval distribution to test cross-benchmark generalization
SemEval uses the official Task 1 test set, not training prompts, according to the paper
All contestants are evaluated on the same prompts, which controls comparative prompt exposure within the tournament but does not decontaminate models
contamination_risk.basis: The evaluated benchmarks are described as publicly available, and the paper reports no decontamination test, no model-specific training-data audit, and no discussion of benchmark or prompt contamination. Because models include later proprietary and open-weight systems with undisclosed training data, prior exposure to public prompts or benchmark distributions cannot be ruled out. The task is generative and comparative rather than answer-key recall, and HTB held-out prompts reduce but do not eliminate contamination concerns.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: The main leaderboard rankings, GPT-5/HumorGen placement claims, cross-judge stability results, Swiss budget-fidelity conclusions, and GTVH feature/failure-mode profiles are determined primarily by Llama and Qwen LLM judgments.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: Small directional validation: three invited annotators produced 175 votes on 60 stratified SemEval pairs, with 55 pairs having all three votes. The paper explicitly treats human annotations as directional agreement evidence rather than ground truth; GTVH feature tags were not separately human-audited.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: The headline empirical conclusions rest on LLM-as-judge pairwise preferences aggregated with BT MLE. Human evaluation is limited in size and validates agreement patterns only, not the full tournament or feature labels.

reporting_gaps: No exact API snapshots, deployment IDs, provider endpoints, checkpoint hashes, or inference dates are reported for the contestant models or LLM judges.

Access mode is not mapped per model; the paper mentions local and API-based models but does not specify which contestant used which access path. Only the primary Llama judge pipeline is tied to H100 hardware.

Candidate-generation token limits are reported inconsistently: the Reproducibility Statement says token limits were inherited from respective model APIs without artificial truncation, while Appendix G Table 19 reports Max New Tokens = 256.
Candidate-generation total calls, latency, retry/failure rates, and monetary API costs are not reported.

The paper says prompt order is swapped across comparisons but does not fully specify the balancing/randomization procedure; reported match counts correspond to one judgment per unordered pair per prompt, not an explicitly duplicated A/B and B/A design.

Human annotator demographics are limited to student status, English proficiency, and coursework/research exposure; age, gender, cultural background, geography, and ethics/IRB handling are not reported.

The human-evaluation vote accounting has limited granularity: the paper reports 175 votes, 60 pairs, and 55 fully overlapping pairs, but does not provide individual annotator vote counts in the main design description.
GTVH mechanism, delivery, and failure tags are co-emitted by the same LLM judge response that decides the duel and are not separately human-audited.
The early verbose chain-of-thought pairwise pilot is reported only as a pilot with approximately 62% tie rate; its judge model and full sample details are not fully specified.
The paper does not discuss training-data or benchmark contamination and reports no decontamination tests for SemEval, HTB, contestant models, or LLM judges.
It is not always explicit whether Swiss 2RR/3RR budget ablations involved fresh LLM calls or simulated/subsampled labels from the full round-robin match history.
